elements.sent-2411-001: Time delay estimation or Time-Difference-Of-Arrival estimates is a critical component for multiple localization applications such as multilateration, direction of arrival, and self-calibration.
elements.sent-2411-002: The task is to estimate the time difference between a signal arriving at two different sensors.
elements.sent-2411-003: For the audio sensor modality, most current systems are based on classical methods such as the Generalized Cross-Correlation Phase Transform (GCC-PHAT) method.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper we demonstrate that learning based methods can--- \change{even based on synthetic data ---significantly outperform GCC-PHAT on novel real world data.}
elements.sent-2411-005: To overcome the lack of data with ground truth for the task, we train our model on a simulated dataset which is sufficiently large and varied, and that captures the relevant characteristics of the real world problem.
elements.sent-2411-006: We provide our trained model, SONNET (Simulation Optimized Neural Network Estimator of Timeshifts), which is runnable in real-time and works on novel data out of the box for many real data applications\change{, i.e. without re-training.}
elements.sent-2411-007: We further demonstrate greatly improved performance on the downstream task of self-calibration when using our model compared to classical methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: Time Delay Estimation (TDE) is the problem of determining how much later (or earlier) a signal from a transmitter is received at two different receivers.
elements.sent-2411-009: The result is often denoted Time-Difference-Of-Arrival (TDOA), see Fig~\ref{fig:TDOA .}
elements.sent-2411-010: TDE is a pivotal problem, primarily due to its critical role in localization and positioning systems.
elements.sent-2411-011: By enabling the determination of the TDOA of a signal at different receivers, TDE provides the foundational measurements for inferring the spatial location of senders and/or receivers using further methods.
elements.sent-2411-013: Multilateration, where the positions of the receivers are known and the TDOA \change{estimates are used to estimate the position of the sender, \cite{GustafssonRT03_11,larsson_optimal_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Direction of arrival, where prior knowledge of the geometry of a receiver array is used together with TDOA measurements from multiple pairs of receiver to compute from which direction the signal is received.
elements.sent-2411-015: Self-calibration, where the positions of both receivers and senders are \change{estimated solely based on the measured TDOA, \cite{zhayida2014automatic,larsson2021fast,tegler_eusipco_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Accurate localization of sender and receiver nodes is crucial for various applications, including microphone array calibration, speaker diarization, beam-forming, radio antenna array calibration, mapping, and positioning \cite{plinge2016acoustic .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In these and many other applications, the initial signal processing step of obtaining reliable TDOA estimates, plays an important role.
elements.sent-2411-018: Currently, the state-of-the-art method is the Generalized Cross-Correlation Phase Transform (GCC-PHAT) and its variants \cite{Knapp1976 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, recent research indicates that there is substantial room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-020: In \cite{astrom2021extenstion it was shown that the average performance of existing methods fell below the desired threshold in nearly 40\% of estimations based on a real dataset with ground truth.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The TDE problem is relevant across multiple different signal modalities such as audio and radio.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, in this paper the main focus is directed towards the analysis of audio, although \change{studying radio signals is an interesting subject for future studies.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/tdoa_example.png} \caption{Since the microphones are at different distances from the speaker, the signal arrives at different times for each of them. By estimating the timeshift in the signals (right figure), and combining it with the propagation speed of the signal, we get a measurement of distance difference (left figure).} \label{fig:TDOA}
elements.sent-2411-023: TDE in the audio domain presents a set of challenges that significantly complicates the estimation process.
elements.sent-2411-024: Unlike scenarios involving controlled signal transmission, for a variety of audio applications the sound source is not a controlled entity.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore our estimation techniques need to handle unknown signals with unpredictable characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-026: This difficulty is compounded by reverberations, a common phenomenon in acoustic spaces where sound waves reflect off surfaces, creating multiple delayed echoes that can obscure the true signal path.
elements.sent-2411-027: Another challenging aspect arises when dealing with moving sound sources, as this introduces a dynamic element to the TDE problem \cite{flood2019stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-028: As the source moves, the relative distances to the receivers change continuously, altering the TDOA in real-time and demanding adaptive estimation techniques capable of handling these variations.
elements.sent-2411-029: Together, these factors—unknown signal characteristics, reverberations, and source mobility—make audio-based TDE a particularly demanding task, necessitating sophisticated algorithms and approaches to achieve reliable estimation.
elements.sent-2411-030: Previous methods for TDE in audio signal processing exhibit notable limitations in handling complex real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-031: The Generalized Cross-Correlation method, and specifically its Phase Transform variant GCC-PHAT is often used as a starting point.
elements.sent-2411-032: It is robust to measurement noise and works well across diverse signal types.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, GCC-PHAT shows limitations when dealing with reverberations and moving sound sources.
elements.sent-2411-034: In \cite{flood2019stochastic methods were developed for estimation of TDOA with sound source or receiver motions.}
elements.sent-2411-035: These methods were based on local optimization of initial estimates based on the GCC-PHAT.
elements.sent-2411-036: Recent advancements have seen the adoption of machine learning techniques for TDE.
elements.sent-2411-037: For small baseline receiver arrays, where the receivers are typically placed equidistant, direction of arrival can be made using alternative techniques, for example steered-response power with phase transform \cite{diaz2020robust , spectrograms \cite{wang2023fn and raw waveforms \cite{he2022sounddoa .}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: For DOA estimation both traditional and data driven methods have been used successfully.
elements.sent-2411-039: For large baseline arrays, where the receivers are placed in an ad-hoc fashion there has been some attempts at data-driven methods, see for example \cite{vera2018towards,grinstein2023dual,grinstein2023graph,gong2022end,feng2023soft and \cite{grumiaux2022survey for an extensive overview.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Many of these are, however, trained and evaluated for specific subtasks.
elements.sent-2411-041: One of the problems has been the lack of real world data with accurate ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-042: Previous work circumvents this by using simulated data to train their models.
elements.sent-2411-043: While this approach is promising, these papers make no claim to have a model which works on novel real world data.
elements.sent-2411-044: Instead, only training and evaluating on similar simulated datasets.
elements.sent-2411-045: In this paper we propose more careful modeling of the problem, and thereby improving over previous learning methods by increasing the scope and quality of the simulations.
elements.sent-2411-046: Many learning based approaches focus primarily on reverberation effects, neglecting other critical factors.
elements.sent-2411-047: Thus, limiting the generalizability of these methods to real data.
elements.sent-2411-048: This paper aims to contribute to the body of knowledge on TDE by leveraging simulated data to explore and enhance estimation techniques for audio signals.
elements.sent-2411-050: Demonstrating that data driven models trained on large scale simulated sound \change{datasets , generalize to real data as well as to novel sounds for the TDE task.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Providing a model, SONNET - Simulation Optimized Neural Network Estimator of Timeshifts, which outperforms state of the art methods for TDE and is evaluated on both simulated and real data.\footnote{Code available at: \url{https://vision.maths.lth.se/sonnet/.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Demonstrating how the new estimators improve performance on downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-053: Consider a reverberant room containing two receivers positioned at $\rec_1,\rec_2 \in \mathbb{R ^3$ and a moving sender located at $\send(t) \in \mathbb{R ^3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The sender is emitting an unknown signal $\signal (t)$ which is being recorded by the receiver at $\rec_i$ as the signal $x_i(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-055: The TDOA at time $t$ for receivers $i$ and $j$, is defined as \begin{equation}\Delta (t) = \frac{||\rec_i - \send(t)|| - ||\rec_j - \send(t)||}{v_x},\end{equation} where $v_x$ is the propagation speed of the signal.
elements.sent-2411-056: We are in this paper primarily interested in this direct path TDOA, but an interesting extension would be to also consider TDOA measurements corresponding to multi-path components from reflective planes, that could potentially provide richer information, \cite{dokmanic2014localize,zhayida2016automatic .}
elements.sent-2411-057: For the modeling we assume that the received signal $x_i(t)$ can be modeled as \begin{equation}x_i(t) = \int h_i(t - \tau,\tau)\signal(\tau)d\tau + \epsilon_i(t),\end{equation} where $\epsilon$ is the noise and $h_i(t, \tau)$ is the impulse response from the sender to receiver $i$ at the position $\send (\tau)$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The impulse response captures the acoustic properties (position, orientation) of both the receiver and sender as well as the reverberant properties of the room.
elements.sent-2411-059: Typically there is a strong direct path component in the impulse response, corresponding to a time-delay of $\frac{||\rec_i - \send|| {v_x}$, which allows for the TDOA estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-060: While the TDOA is time dependent, we will for the rest of this paper refer to the TDOA of a pair recorded signals $\signal_i(t), \signal_j(t), 0 < t < T$ as the TDOA value at the middle of the signal, i.e. $\Delta (\frac{T {2 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The goal is to use two recorded signals $\signal_i, \signal_j$ to estimate this TDOA value.
elements.sent-2411-062: Similar to earlier work by Berg et al \cite{berg22_interspeech , we use Pyroomacoustics \cite{scheibler2018pyroomacoustics to compute impulse responses using the image source method \cite{allen1979image .}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: However, we augment the simulation in a number of important ways.
elements.sent-2411-064: Instead of simulating a single room we simulate a broader class of rooms in order to cover a larger set of possible impulse responses.
elements.sent-2411-065: Also, with the goal of making the simulation better reflect reality, we both simulate a moving sound source and also microphones and sound sources which are not omnidirectional.
elements.sent-2411-066: How and why will be explained in more detail in the following sections and motivated by our ablation study in section~\ref{exp:abl .}
elements.sent-2411-067: To simulate a moving sound source we first generate a path by constructing a quadratic Bézier curve $s(t), \, 0 < t < T$ with a length shorter than some maximum length.
elements.sent-2411-068: Because simulating the sound from a moving sound source is difficult within the Pyroomacoustics framework, we instead discretize the curve into $k$ points \begin{equation}\lbrace s(t_1), \dots , s(t_k) \rbrace, \quad t_i = \frac{i-1}{k-1}T.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-069: The sound from a moving sound source is then approximated by dividing the played sound $x(t)$ into $k$ equally sized parts and simulating part $x(t), \frac{i-1 {k T < t < \frac{i {k T$ as a stationary speaker at point $s(t_i)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: This is essentially simulating that the sound source is jumping to a new location along a path after each time $\frac{T {k}$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Following this methodology, the signal is computed as a sum of convolutions \begin{equation}\signal_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} h_i(t, \frac{j}{k}) * \bar{x}^{(j)}(t),\end{equation} where $\bar{x ^{(j)}$ is the $j$th part of the signal, zero-padded to have the same shape as the original signal $\signal$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Previous work simulated both \change{microphones and speakers as being omnidirectional, which means they emit/receive their signal equally well in all directions.}
elements.sent-2411-073: However, the directional dependence in reality is rather complicated, since it depends on what hardware is used.
elements.sent-2411-074: This is further complicated by the directionality of a microphone not being constant for all frequencies, as demonstrated in \cite{zetterqvist2023using .}
elements.sent-2411-075: We settled on using the subcardioid sensitivity pattern, since it is a common model for directional microphones and therefore already implemented in Pyroomacoustics \change{\cite{de2011generalized .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: One of the main contributions of this paper is to show that it is possible to close the sim2real gap, i.e.\ to demonstrate that simulated data generalizes to real data, given enough simulations of sufficiently rich character.
elements.sent-2411-077: Therefore we opted for a network architecture with the properties of simplicity and being easily trainable, see Fig \ref{fig:net_arch .}
elements.sent-2411-078: The main part of our network is a ResNet, since it is a simple architecture which is easily trainable.
elements.sent-2411-079: However, an issue with audio data is that it has low information density, making it difficult to input the audio data directly into the ResNet, without significantly increasing the number of parameters of the model.
elements.sent-2411-080: We therefore use two common approaches to compress the audio data.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/network_architecture.png} \caption{System overview: Our model takes two audio recordings of length $d$ as input data. The data is first converted to the frequency domain, using the fast Fourier transform, and stored with real and imaginary components as different channels. It is then sent through a series of 1d convolutional layers. The features are then processed using $M$ stacked pairs of linear layers along with skip connections. Finally, the logits are acquired by adding a linear layer after the last residual block.} \label{fig:net_arch}
elements.sent-2411-081: First we use a fast Fourier transform and only store the values for frequencies which are below some threshold frequency $f_{max}$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Another advantage of using the Fourier transform is that it mitigates the problem of the spectral bias in neural networks \cite{rahaman2019spectral .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Secondly, we use a backbone of 1d convolutional layers to extract more dense features from the data.
elements.sent-2411-084: In the same manner as \cite{berg22_interspeech our model performs regression-via-classification (RvC).}
elements.sent-2411-085: This means that our network does not output TDOA values, but rather outputs logits for a fixed number of classes.
elements.sent-2411-086: Each of the classes then corresponds to a range of TDOA values.
elements.sent-2411-087: To make the data have the right size we use linear layers as projections between the backbone and the ResNet, as well as a linear classifier between the ResNet and the final logits.
elements.sent-2411-088: In our code we have provided a pretrained model SONNET.
elements.sent-2411-089: In this section we have outlined implementation details on both how SONNET was trained, as well as how the dataset it was trained on was generated.
elements.sent-2411-090: The sound used when training the model are from the Musan dataset \cite{musan2015 , which is an openly available corpus containing about 18 GB of music, speech and noise.}
elements.sent-2411-091: For our simulations we used a signal length of $d$=10,000 samples with a sampling frequency of 16 kHz.
elements.sent-2411-092: However, since we want reverberations to be present at the beginning of the recording we simulated 2000 extra samples at the beginning of each simulation.
elements.sent-2411-093: For each recording, we simulated a new room in the shape of a rectangular cuboid with each of the three dimensions having a length uniformly sampled in the interval $[1,10]$ m.
elements.sent-2411-094: The reverberation level in the room was varied by sampling the reflection coefficient of the walls from the interval $[0.05,0.99]$.
elements.sent-2411-095: The path of the sender was simulated in one of two different ways with equal probability, either as a stationary point source or as a randomly sampled quadratic Bézier curve with maximum velocity of 5 m/s.
elements.sent-2411-096: In each room we simulated 50 microphones recording the signal.
elements.sent-2411-097: The dataset consist of 10,000 rooms, which means that it in total contains $10,000 \binom{50 {2 = 12$ million training examples of pairs of recordings.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: The memory footprint of the dataset is 19 GB.
elements.sent-2411-099: For the model we set our threshold frequency $f_{max = 4800$ Hz.}
elements.sent-2411-100: The backbone consisted of three 1d convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-102: Throughout the network we used GELU \cite{hendrycks2016gaussian as activation function.}
elements.sent-2411-103: We choose to have 1000 output classes as possible predictions for our model.
elements.sent-2411-104: Each class corresponded to bins of TDOA values with a width of 1 sample.
elements.sent-2411-105: This means our model makes predictions with the same resolution as GCC-PHAT.
elements.sent-2411-106: The model was trained in PyTorch \cite{Paszke_PyTorch_An_Imperative_2019 with the AdamW optimizer \cite{loshchilov2017decoupled , a batch size of 4096, a learning rate of 0.0003, during 20 epochs.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: We used the cross-entropy loss with label-smoothing of 0.1 as our loss function.
elements.sent-2411-108: The training was done on Tesla V100-PCIE-16GB GPU and took 3 hours.
elements.sent-2411-109: In this section we first analyze the inference speed and memory footprint of the proposed system.
elements.sent-2411-110: We then study the performance of the system on both simulated and real data, \change{by making several comparative studies against GCC-PHAT .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Finally we show how the proposed system can be used to improve a previous state-of-the-art system for automatic self-calibration of an ad-hoc configuration of microphones.
elements.sent-2411-112: We quantitatively and qualitatively (see Figures~\ref{fig:sensitivity , \ref{fig:acc_at and \ref{fig:qual_res ) show that our model outperforms previous methods for performing TDE in novel real world settings.}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: The ideas in this paper can be used train TDE models of different sizes, and can therefore be tailored to the available memory and computation requirements for a specific use case.
elements.sent-2411-114: However, we suggest as a starting point to use SONNET, which we have provided along with this paper.
elements.sent-2411-115: SONNET has 20 million parameters and a memory footprint of 75 MB.
elements.sent-2411-116: Inference speed was evaluated on both CPU, Intel(R) Xeon(R) W-2125 CPU @ 4.00GHz, and on a GPU, Tesla V100-PCIE-16GB.
elements.sent-2411-117: The results are shown in Table \ref{tab:inference_speed .}
elements.sent-2411-118: To summarize, SONNET takes four times longer to run than GCC-PHAT, however, the inference time is still fast enough to run SONNET on a CPU in real-time without any issues.
elements.figure-2411-003: [] \caption{Computation time per pair using a batch size of 100 recording pairs.} \label{tab:inference_speed} \begin{tabular}{lcc} SONNET (ms) GCC-PHAT (ms) CPU 0.94 0.32 GPU 0.022 0.005 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-119: We have also evaluated our model's robustness to noise and reverberation using simulated data, see Fig.~\ref{fig:sensitivity .}
elements.sent-2411-120: We use accuracy at 10 cm as our main evaluation metric.
elements.sent-2411-121: To motivate this, we would like to highlight the distribution of the residuals.
elements.sent-2411-122: The residual distribution for all three detectors shown here seems to be well explained by a combination of a normal distribution (inliers) and a uniform distribution (outliers), a common combination of distributions in the area of robust estimation.
elements.sent-2411-123: Because of this, using mean squared error as an evaluation metric is both noisy and highly dependent on the space of values the model can estimate making comparisons between models more difficult.
elements.sent-2411-124: When using these detections for downstream tasks, because they contain outliers one probably need to use methods from robust estimation.
elements.sent-2411-125: Because of this, we think reporting inlier ratio, at a given inlier threshold, is a better evaluation metric.
elements.sent-2411-126: The evaluation examples are created in the same manner as the training data, with the change that instead of using audio from Musan we used the audio from tdoa\_20201016 (described in Section\ref{sec:real-data ) in the simulation.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Our model outperforms GCC-PHAT in a wide range of reverberant and noise environments\change{, as shown in Fig~\ref{fig:sensitivity .}}

(a) Noise sensitivity evaluated at $T_{60}$~=~0.2~s. Note that GCC-PHAT is very robust against white noise (b) Reverberation sensitivity evaluated at SNR~=~10~dB} \label{fig:sensitivity}
elements.sent-2411-128: Arguably, the most important evaluation of our model is the results on real world data.
elements.sent-2411-129: We have evaluated our model on the tdoa\_20201016 dataset provided by \cite{astrom2021extenstion .}
elements.sent-2411-130: The advantage of using this dataset is that it contains ground truth values for the TDOA for any pair of two microphones.
elements.sent-2411-131: The dataset also contains recordings without accompanying ground truth but these were not used in our evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-132: The dataset is recorded in 96 kHz, which means that we have to down-sample, since our model is trained on 16 kHz.
elements.sent-2411-133: The total playing time of the speaker over all the experiments is around 600 s with 12 microphones recording.
elements.sent-2411-134: Using a window overlap of 5/6, we get 384648 pairs of windows to estimate TDOA on.
elements.sent-2411-135: As shown in Fig~\ref{fig:acc_at , the learned models significantly outperforms GCC-PHAT.}
elements.sent-2411-136: We also show qualitative results over some of the recordings in the dataset in Fig~\ref{fig:qual_res .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Since the main reason for studying TDE is its use in downstream applications, we have evaluated our models on the task of self-calibration using the \cite{astrom2021extenstion dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-138: In self-calibration the goal is to estimate the 3D geometry of both the receivers and senders using only the TDOA values as input, i.e. no prior position information.
elements.sent-2411-139: To do this, we used the TDOA values acquired as input to a published self-calibration system \cite{larsson2021fast,astrom2021extenstion .}
elements.sent-2411-140: We then compare our estimated 3D positions with the ground truth positions provided in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-141: In order to be able to do this comparison, we need to fix the gauge freedom in the solution, in this case the solution and ground truth might differ by a Euclidean transformation.
elements.sent-2411-142: We estimate this Euclidean transformation using the receiver positions of our solution compared to the ground truth receiver positions.
elements.sent-2411-143: This is similar to how to evaluate maps found using Structure-from-Motion (SfM) or Simultaneous Localization And Mapping (SLAM).
elements.sent-2411-144: After applying the Euclidean transformation to the solution, the residuals are then computed as the distances between corresponding receivers in the solution and ground truth.
elements.figure-2411-007: [] \caption{RMS error of the receivers for the estimated 3D geometry after registration to the ground truth. Estimation is done using TDOA values from SONNET or GCC-PHAT. Experiments missing a value have an error larger than 1 m.} \label{tab:downstream-rms} \begin{tabular}{l@{ 6pt}c} Experiment SONNET (m) GCC-PHAT (m) chirp\_0001 0.05 0.80 chirp\_0002 0.05 0.17 chirp\_0004 0.05 0.40 iregchirp\_0006 0.06 0.54 iregchirp\_0007 0.04 0.59 music\_0008 0.07 -- music\_0009 0.06 -- music\_0010 0.04 0.31 music\_0011 0.05 -- music\_0012 0.04 -- music\_0013 0.03 0.35 music\_0014 0.04 0.28 music\_0015 0.04 0.10 metronom\_0021 0.16 -- metronom\_0022 0.10 -- median 0.05 0.59 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-145: As can be seen in Table \ref{tab:downstream-rms , using the TDOA values from our learned models makes the self-calibration system converge to good solutions on all of the experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-146: This is a significant improvement compared to using GCC-PHAT for which the system only manages to converge on some of the experiments and even when it converges it has larger errors.
elements.sent-2411-147: An example of a 3D reconstruction resulting from using the learned model together with the self-calibration system can be seen in Fig \ref{fig:3d-reconstruct .}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{images/3d_reconstruction.png} \caption{Example of 3D reconstruction, on the experiment music\_0014} \label{fig:3d-reconstruct}
elements.sent-2411-148: Earlier works which use simulated data to train models to solve TDE have not been demonstrated to generalize to real world data.
elements.sent-2411-149: We claim that we can achieve a good generalization to novel real world data by: scaling the dataset, simulating sound source with movement and directionality.
elements.sent-2411-150: To show that these three changes are helpful we have performed two ablation studies.
elements.sent-2411-151: In the first ablation study we changed how the dataset was generated by including or excluding the simulation augmentations: moving the sound source or directionality of the sound source.
elements.sent-2411-152: For each of the four configuration we trained a separate model in the same way as the full SONNET model.
elements.sent-2411-153: Each of the models were then evaluated on tdoa\_20201016 in the same way as in section \ref{sec:real-data , and the results are shown in Fig \ref{fig:ablation_aug .}}
elements.sent-2411-154: As we can see, using learning based methods on stationary omnidirectional data outperforms GCC-PHAT.
elements.sent-2411-155: However, we can further improve the method by augmenting the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-156: For the second ablation study, we trained models on different sizes of the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-157: The models were then evaluated on tdoa\_20201016 in the same way as in section \ref{sec:real-data , the results are shown in Fig \ref{fig:ablation_dataset .}}
elements.sent-2411-158: As we can see, having a large enough dataset is important for generalization and scaling up the dataset might be a way to improve the model further.
elements.sent-2411-159: As we have demonstrated in this paper, combining the ability to simulate data with learning based methods is a promising direction for further studies.
elements.sent-2411-160: In this paper we have shown that it is possible to improve on TDE, a key task when performing audio based localization.
elements.sent-2411-161: However, using simulation together with learning based methods is also a ripe area for further studies, since it enables an approach to harder versions of the TDE problem.
elements.sent-2411-162: Such examples include using multiple sound sources, multipath components of the sound, or utilizing the information from more than two microphones at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-163: Since we can simulate such data with ground truth, it might be possible to create detectors for such problems.
elements.sent-2411-164: It is our belief that this paper is a key stepping stone for further studies in the area.. \bibliographystyle{splncs04.}
